summary: I was born and raised in Nashville, Tennessee, the oldest of eight children. Growing up here during the Great Depression and into the 1940s, my family was middle-class and Roman Catholic. Nashville has always been a city with a relatively small black population compared to other Southern cities, and I lived a somewhat sheltered life here. My uncle was an executive at one of the Nashville newspapers, which helped me get started in journalism after World War II. Politics and local stories from Nashville deeply shaped my early experiences.

My father was a contractor who nearly went broke during the Great Depression, which was a tough time for our family economically. Despite the hardships, we managed to maintain a middle-class lifestyle in Nashville through the 1930s and into the 1940s. The Depression shaped much of my early childhood and gave me a perspective on economic struggles in the South. It was a backdrop to my upbringing before I went off to serve in World War II.

After returning from World War II, I enrolled at Peabody College in Nashville with the intention of becoming a teacher. However, through my uncle's connections at the local newspaper, I started working there as a cub reporter and part-time copy boy while attending school. Peabody was the place where my career path shifted from education to journalism, and it was during this time that I really got involved with the newspaper. My time at Peabody overlapped with my early reporting years in the late 1940s.

I grew up in a traditional Roman Catholic family in Nashville, with my mother being Irish and my father German-Irish. Our faith was an important part of our identity, and holidays like St. Patrick's Day were big celebrations for us. Although being Catholic in the South sometimes marked us as different, I never personally felt persecuted or faced prejudice because of it. Roman Catholicism was a foundation in my family life and shaped many of our traditions and values.

In my family, after Christmas, St. Patrick's Day was the biggest holiday we celebrated, reflecting our Irish heritage. My mother, being Irish, made sure it was a special occasion each year. Growing up in Nashville, these celebrations were a strong part of our family tradition and identity. It was a time when our Roman Catholic faith and cultural background came together in meaningful ways.

During World War II, I served as a control tower operator in the United States Air Force. From my vantage point, I observed the strict racial segregation within the military, which was striking and made me aware of the deep racial divides of the time. I noticed how black troops marched with pride and precision, which contrasted with the whites, and this observation stayed with me. My service in the Air Force was a formative experience that influenced my later views on racial injustice.

I served in World War II as a control tower operator in the Air Force, which was a pivotal period in my life. The war exposed me to new realities, including the racial segregation in the military and the broader social issues of the time. After the war, I returned to Nashville and began college at Peabody, eventually starting my career in journalism. The experiences from the war deeply shaped my perspectives and motivated my later work on social issues.

I remember hearing black troops singing 'Mammy' as they marched during my time in the Air Force in World War II. The song was part of the cultural expression I observed, and it highlighted the rich traditions within the black community despite the segregation and inequities they faced. These moments stayed with me as I reflected on racial injustice. It was a small but powerful example of the pride and spirit of black soldiers during the war.

In 1945 or 1946, when my sister was a finalist in a statewide speech contest that included black participants for the first time, my mother was concerned about hosting them since they were coming from Memphis, Tennessee. We ended up inviting two black contestants to our home for a party, which caused quite a stir at the time. Memphis was part of the broader regional context for racial issues that were beginning to affect my family and me. This event was an early personal encounter with racial integration and hospitality.

I started working at the Nashville Banner in 1948 as a copy boy and part-time reporter while attending Peabody College. My uncle, who was vice-president and circulation director for both Nashville newspapers, helped me get this job. I became a full-time reporter by 1949 and eventually rose to editor by 1962. The Banner was central to my career and gave me a platform to explore social and political issues in Nashville.

Boss Crump was a powerful political figure who controlled much of Nashville's politics through his agent Bill Jones. My family was connected to this political machine; my uncle Louis was Bill Jones's son-in-law and worked in state government, with his job often depending on Crump's political success. Crump's influence was a constant presence in local politics during my youth, shaping the political landscape of Nashville. Understanding this helped me grasp the nature of local power and patronage.

Bill Jones was Nashville's agent for Boss Crump and effectively ran the city's political operations. My uncle Louis was married to Bill Jones's daughter and worked in state government under this political structure. Jones was seen as the boss of Boss Crump in town, and his influence was significant in determining political appointments and elections. This connection gave my family a close-up view of Nashville's political workings during the mid-1900s.

My uncle Louis, my father's brother, was deeply involved in Nashville politics through his connection as Bill Jones's son-in-law. He held a state government job that was often at risk depending on the political fortunes of Boss Crump's machine. Later in life, Louis worked in the press room at the Nashville Banner, where I was editor, and I helped him return to work there. He was a significant figure in my family's political and professional life, bridging local politics and journalism.

Gene Woodruff worked for my father when he was a contractor in Nashville. Gene had a brother named Horace Woodruff, whose criminal case and death row experience became a notable story in my family. My father and other relatives tried to intervene on Horace's behalf with the governor to save his life. Gene's connection to my family brought me into contact with these dramatic events and influenced my later interest in prison reform.

Horace Woodruff was the brother of Gene Woodruff, who worked for my father. Horace was convicted of killing a policeman and sentenced to death, but my family intervened and helped spare his life. Later, he led a prison break that resulted in multiple deaths, and he spent twelve years on death row. Despite his past, I became an intimate friend of Horace when I was reporting on prison reform and visited the prison where he worked in the radio shop. His story was deeply intertwined with my family's history and my journalistic work.

Mike Mulvehill was a policeman who was killed by Horace Woodruff, the brother of Gene Woodruff who worked for my father. This tragic event was a significant moment in my family's history, leading to efforts to save Horace from the electric chair. The killing and its aftermath were often discussed in my family, highlighting the complex and sometimes dark intersections of crime and justice we witnessed. Mulvehill's death was a catalyst for my family's involvement in prison-related issues.

Lonnie Taylor was an inmate who lost his arm during a prison break led by Horace Woodruff. This violent event was part of the grim realities of the prison system I later reported on. The incident underscored the dangers and challenges within the penal system, which became a focus of my journalistic work. Taylor's injury was a reminder of the human cost behind the headlines.

Droupie Edwards was the warden of the prison where I met Horace Woodruff, who ran the radio shop there. Edwards facilitated my access to the prison and introduced me to Horace, which helped me develop a close relationship with him. This connection was important as I pursued stories on prison reform during my reporting career. Edwards played a key role in bridging the gap between the prison administration and my journalistic efforts.

Boots Brown was a veterinarian and local political candidate whom my grandfather helped during an election. I remember my grandfather telling a humorous story about a parade where Boots's opponent, who had no legs, was paraded through town in a buggy. My grandfather was a precinct politico who was deeply involved in local elections, and Boots Brown's campaign was one of the many political tales from my family's history. These stories gave me insight into the colorful and sometimes rough nature of Nashville politics.
